title: Signed Numbers and Two's Complement
summary: Represent negative numbers in binary using sign-magnitude, one's complement, and two's complement.
content_html:
Why Signed Numbers?

Computers perform arithmetic on both positive and negative integers. The representation scheme determines how arithmetic is implemented in hardware. Two main approaches exist: sign-magnitude and two's complement.

Sign-Magnitude Representation

The most significant bit (MSB) encodes the sign: 0 = positive, 1 = negative. The remaining bits store the magnitude.

8-bit sign-magnitude:
+6  = 0 0000110
-6  = 1 0000110
     ^--- sign bit

Steps to Find Sign-Magnitude

  1. Convert the absolute value to binary using N-1 bits
  2. Prepend 0 if positive; prepend 1 if negative
Example: +70 in 8-bit sign-magnitude
  70 = 1000110  (7 bits)
  Positive → prepend 0 → 01000110

Example: -70 in 8-bit sign-magnitude
  70 = 1000110
  Negative → prepend 1 → 11000110

Sign-Magnitude Trade-offs

  • + Simple and intuitive
  • + Easy to implement
  • − Two representations of zero: +0 = 0000 0000 and −0 = 1000 0000
  • − Addition/subtraction require special-case logic
  • − Reduced value range (one bit is 'wasted' on sign)

Two's Complement Representation

Two's complement solves sign-magnitude problems by shifting the number range so that 0 is in the middle. For N bits, the value range is [−2N−1, 2N−1 − 1].

8-bit range:  −128 to +127
16-bit range: −32,768 to +32,767
32-bit range: −2,147,483,648 to +2,147,483,647

Converting to Two's Complement (Negative Numbers)

  1. Write the binary representation of the positive value (N bits)
  2. Invert all bits (bitwise NOT / complement)
  3. Add 1
Example: −6 in 8-bit two's complement
  Step 1:  6 = 0000 0110
  Step 2:  Complement → 1111 1001
  Step 3:  Add 1      → 1111 1010
  Result:  -6 = 1111 1010

Example: −72 in 8-bit two's complement
  Step 1:  72 = 0100 1000
  Step 2:  Complement → 1011 0111
  Step 3:  Add 1      → 1011 1000
  Result:  -72 = 1011 1000

Positive Numbers in Two's Complement

Positive numbers are identical to unsigned binary. MSB = 0 always.

Example: +72 = 0100 1000  (unchanged)

Two's Complement Trade-offs

  • + Memory efficient — retains full representational capacity
  • + MSB still indicates sign (0 = positive, 1 = negative)
  • + Zero has a single unique representation (all 0s)
  • + Addition works identically for positive and negative — same hardware
  • + Subtraction: A − B = A + two's_complement(B)
  • − Slightly more complex to convert (handled automatically by ALU)

Two's Complement Addition

The carry out from the MSB is simply discarded.

Example: 6 + (−6) = 0
  0000 0110  (+6)
+ 1111 1010  (−6 in 2's complement)
-----------
1 0000 0000  → discard carry → 0000 0000 = 0 ✓

Example: 5 + (−3) = 2
  0000 0101  (+5)
+ 1111 1101  (−3 in 2's complement)
-----------
1 0000 0010  → discard carry → 2 ✓

Sign Extension

When moving a signed value to a larger register (e.g., 8-bit → 16-bit), the sign bit is repeated to fill the new higher-order bits. This preserves the value exactly.

−16 in 8-bit two's complement:  1111 0000
Extended to 16-bit:  1111 1111  1111 0000  (repeat the 1 sign bit)

+16 in 8-bit:  0001 0000
Extended to 16-bit:  0000 0000  0001 0000  (repeat the 0 sign bit)
